title: Meta Contractors Posed as Teens to Prompt Rival Chatbots About Suicide, Sex, and Drugs
gist: Meta contractor Covalen ran a secret, months-long project ('Cannes') in which hundreds of workers posed as minors to bombard OpenAI's ChatGPT, Google's Gemini, and Character.AI with over 45,000 harmful prompts, raising governance questions about whether 'safety benchmarking' is being used as anticompetitive cover.

themes.theme: Scale and mechanics of the Cannes project

insights.claim: Meta contractor Covalen ran a project called Cannes, active as recently as April 21, 2026, in which hundreds of contractors created dummy under-18 accounts to probe how OpenAI's ChatGPT, Google's Gemini, and Character.AI responded to prompts about suicide, sex, eating disorders, and other high-risk subjects.
insights.speaker: Dhruv Mehrotra
insights.so_what: A trillion-dollar company secretly red-teaming named competitors using fake minor identities is a governance and legal exposure event that could trigger regulatory scrutiny or competitor litigation.
insights.quote: Hundreds of contractors working on a project for Meta were instructed to pose as minors online and probe how competitor chatbots responded to prompts involving suicide, sex, eating disorders, and other high-risk subjects

insights.claim: A single round of testing completed in August 2025 involved more than 45,000 prompts run through rival chatbots, none of which the companies being tested were aware of.
insights.speaker: Dhruv Mehrotra
insights.so_what: The 45,000-prompt scale in a single round indicates industrialized, systematic competitor probing rather than ad hoc research — relevant to assessing Meta's real AI safety R&D versus competitive intelligence gathering.
insights.quote: A single round of testing completed in August 2025 saw more than 45,000 prompts run through the rival chatbots. The companies behind the chatbots weren't aware of the testing.

insights.claim: A reviewed spreadsheet of 3,748 prompts showed hundreds focused on suicide/self-harm, hundreds more on eating disorders, and at least 239 involving sex or romance, often written from the perspective of children or teens in crisis.
insights.speaker: Dhruv Mehrotra
insights.so_what: Granular prompt-category breakdown gives a concrete sense of the severity and intent of the testing corpus, useful for assessing legal/reputational risk exposure.
insights.quote: At least 239 involved sex or romance.

insights.claim: An internal Covalen document described the project as 'comprehensive AI safety benchmarking' that delivered 'critical datasets for model comparison and compliance,' but the documents reviewed do not indicate how, or whether, Meta used the collected responses.
insights.speaker: Dhruv Mehrotra
insights.so_what: The ambiguity over end-use of the data is the crux of the governance risk: unclear whether this fed Meta's own model training, which would be a stronger antitrust/ToS-violation signal.
insights.quote: An internal Covalen document described the project as "comprehensive AI safety benchmarking" and said it delivered "critical datasets for model comparison and compliance."

insights.claim: Rumman Chowdhury, CEO of Humane Intelligence PBC, argues that a monthslong, large-scale project designed to systematically break competitors' safety rules via fake child accounts falls outside what is normally considered 'industry standard' safety evaluation.
insights.speaker: Dhruv Mehrotra
insights.so_what: An independent AI-safety authority explicitly rejects Meta's 'industry standard' framing, undermining the company's defense and strengthening the case for regulatory or ToS enforcement action.
insights.quote: Structuring a monthslong, large-scale project that appears designed to systematically break those rules, via dummy accounts masquerading as children, is outside what is usually described as 'industry standard' evaluation

insights.claim: Chowdhury frames the blending of safety evaluation and competitor benchmarking as 'exactly the kind of governance gray zone where safety becomes a convenient cover for anticompetitive practices.'
insights.speaker: Dhruv Mehrotra
insights.so_what: A reusable mental model — 'safety-washing' — for evaluating whether AI labs' safety-testing claims mask anticompetitive data harvesting; applicable across the AI industry going forward.
insights.quote: exactly the kind of governance gray zone where safety becomes a convenient cover for anticompetitive practices

insights.claim: Meta publicly defended the work as 'routine safety testing' and 'industry-standard practice,' and stated it does not use competitor benchmarking to train its own AI models.
insights.speaker: Dhruv Mehrotra
insights.so_what: Meta's denial of using the data for training is the key factual claim that would need independent verification to assess true competitive/legal risk.
insights.quote: Testing and benchmarking chatbot responses to help ensure safe and age-appropriate experiences is a responsible, industry-standard practice, and any suggestion otherwise completely misunderstands how technology companies work to refine and improve their systems

insights.claim: Precedent exists for competitor benchmarking in AI: Business Insider reported that Scale AI contractors working on Google's Bard compared its responses with ChatGPT outputs and rewrote answers to match or beat them.
insights.speaker: Dhruv Mehrotra
insights.so_what: Establishes that competitor benchmarking itself isn't novel in AI training pipelines, which narrows the actual point of contention to scale, secrecy, and use of simulated-minor identities.

insights.claim: The Cannes project appears to have violated the terms of service of all three targeted companies: OpenAI bars unsolicited safety testing and using outputs to develop competing models; Google prohibits bypassing safety filters outside its own bug-testing programs; Character.AI prohibits harmful or exploitative content and has banned open-ended chat for under-18 users since late 2025.
insights.speaker: Dhruv Mehrotra
insights.so_what: Clear ToS violations across three major AI companies create potential grounds for contract-breach claims or platform bans against Meta/Covalen-linked accounts.
insights.quote: OpenAI bars unsolicited safety testing, efforts to bypass safeguards, and using outputs to 'develop models that compete with OpenAI.'

insights.claim: Character.AI's spokesperson said the company had not authorized the testing and called it a violation of both its Terms of Service and 'the characters and worlds our community has created.'
insights.speaker: Dhruv Mehrotra
insights.so_what: A direct, on-record accusation from a named competitor raises the likelihood of formal legal or platform-access retaliation against Meta/Covalen.
insights.quote: This alleged action is not only a violation of our Terms of Service, but also a violation of the characters and worlds our community has created

insights.claim: Two attorneys specializing in online speech and platform law, Kendra Albert and Riana Pfefferkorn, reviewed sample prompts and said the material did not cross the line into soliciting child sexual abuse material or illegal obscenity.
insights.speaker: Dhruv Mehrotra
insights.so_what: Limits the most severe potential legal exposure (CSAM) even as ToS and ethical violations remain, shaping the realistic range of regulatory/legal outcomes.

insights.claim: Former contractors feared the project could inadvertently generate or preserve child sexual abuse material if a chatbot responded improperly to sexual prompts involving minors, and worried the effort amounted to secretly extracting material from competitors' systems.
insights.speaker: Dhruv Mehrotra
insights.so_what: Internal contractor unease documented on the record strengthens the whistleblower/legal-discovery narrative and signals reputational risk that could surface in future litigation.
insights.quote: I've seen a lot of things I wish I hadn't while doing this job. Everyone I knew who worked on this project was completely gobsmacked by some of the text they were asking us to test.
